old_claim.note: Steam engines supplied mechanical power for later generator deployment, but Faraday's early generator principle did not require steam power.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because self-excitation requires mechanical rotation but does not prescribe a steam engine rather than another prime mover.

source_shape.source_locator: Pages 368-369 describe rotation imparted to the armature, available mechanical force, and a driving strap, without specifying a steam engine as the necessary source of motion.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The primary paper requires mechanical rotation for generation but leaves the prime mover unspecified.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Steam was one historical deployment option; retaining it would elevate one substitutable power source into a causal prerequisite for the dynamo principle.

ontology_before: Dynamo carried Steam Engine as a scaling dependency even though its source note admitted the generator did not require steam.
ontology_after: Self-Excited Industrial Dynamo requires mechanical rotation conceptually but has no direct edge to any one substitutable prime mover.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: dynamo no longer directly depends on steam_engine.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to a specific steam-driven generating installation rather than the self-excited dynamo machine.
A primary source shows the 1866 machine could only be operated by a steam engine.
A generic prime_mover node is added and directly evidenced as a required component of this scope.
short_reason: Steam is a substitutable prime mover, not the dynamo mechanism.
